entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2604
entry.content:
Before we get into this month's update, make sure you’re registered for the upcoming What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2605 webcast, covering the top features from the releases 2603-2605. Alongside the blogs, it gives you live demos and the chance to ask your questions as you go. We’ll be answering them live via chat during the session. Register here.

Asset Reliability Engineering

See what changed (and why) in your recommendations  - New Change Log

When recommendations evolve over time, one critical question often remains unanswered:
What changed, when, and who changed it?

New Change Log tab page in the Manage Recommendations application

The Challenge

Changes to recommendations used to be either overwritten or require manual duplication. This created a gap:

  • No clear audit trail
  • Limited transparency into decision-making
  • Loss of historical context

For reliability engineers and maintenance planners, this made it difficult to track how and why recommendations have evolved, especially in environments where traceability is essential.

The Solution

We introduced a change log/timeline tab page for Recommendations in Manage Recommendations and Implement Recommendations applications.

All changes to recommendation business objects are automatically documented - independent of versioning - and made visible directly in the UI through a dedicated timeline view.

Behind the scenes, change logging is already captured at database level by the SAP Cloud Application Programming framework. This enhancement brings that data to life by exposing it to users in a clear, accessible way.

The Outcome

With the new change log/timeline:

  • Every change is visible
  • Every action is traceable
  • Every decision has context

This enables:

  • Greater transparency across teams
  • Improved auditability and compliance
  • Better-informed decision-making over time

For more information, refer to View and Export Change Logs .

 

Revising a Standalone Recommendation

Maintenance strategies don’t stay right forever. Assets change. Conditions change. Insights improve.
But if every change means overwriting the past, you lose the very context that explains why decisions were made.

And that’s a real issue:
Without history, there’s no true learning, no auditability, and no confidence in how your strategy evolves.

The Challenge

Today, updating a recommendation forces a compromise:

  • Overwrite it and lose historical context
  • Recreate it and break continuity

Over time, this leads to fragmented strategies and limited traceability especially across long asset lifecycles and changing teams.

The Solution

The new Revise button for Standalone Recommendations allows Reliability Engineers to:

  • Create a new version of an implemented recommendation
  • Carry forward all relevant data automatically
  • Track changes between versions with full transparency

New 'Revise' button for an implemented recommendation in the 'Manage Recommendations' application

Instead of disconnected updates, recommendations now evolve as a continuous, structured history.

The Outcome

  • End-to-end traceability of strategy changes
  • Clear audit trail across versions
  • Confidence to adapt without losing context
  • Continuity over decades of asset operation

In a nutshell, change your strategy - without losing your story.

For more information, see Revising a Standalone Recommendation.

 

Consolidation of Recommendations

The Challenge

As recommendations grow across assets, assessments, and teams, a new challenge emerges:
too many similar recommendations, scattered across the system.

Even when they relate to the same issue, you're left:

  • Reviewing and implementing them individually
  • Duplicating effort across similar entries
  • Losing efficiency in planning and execution

Over time, this doesn't just slow things down - it creates unnecessary complexity in your maintenance processes.

With the Consolidate Recommendations feature, you can now merge multiple standalone recommendations into a single, unified entry.

This allows you to bring together recommendations that share common characteristics - such as the same type, subtype, or failure data profile - and manage them as one.

Instead of handling multiple similar recommendations separately, you streamline them into a single action.

The Solution

When consolidating recommendations, the system ensures consistency by applying a few key rules:

  • You can select up to 10 recommendations for consolidation
  • Only recommendations in the following statuses can be selected:
    • Created
    • In Process
    • Ready for Release
    • In Review
  • All selected recommendations must share:
    • The same Type and Subtype
    • The same Failure Data Profile
    • The same Planning Plant and Maintenance Plant
  • For assessment-based recommendations:
    • The Source Assessment must be identical

These checks ensure that only truly comparable recommendations are merged - maintaining data integrity while reducing duplication.

The Outcome

By consolidating recommendations, you:

  • Reduce duplication across similar actions
  • Simplify implementation workflows
  • Improve clarity for planners and engineers
  • Focus effort where it matters most

Instead of managing many similar recommendations, you move forward with one clear, consolidated action. This is a small change with a big impact - helping you keep recommendation management lean, structured, and scalable.

 

Asset Health Monitoring

Interacting with Embedded IoT Platform via Command Line Interface

SAP Asset Performance Management now supports the Command Line Interface of the Embedded IoT platform provider. As a Data Integration engineer, you can use this interface to efficiently work with the platform and automate tasks such as device management. This improves operational efficiency and enables scripted automation of routine tasks.

For more information, refer to Interacting with Embedded IoT Platform via Command Line Interface.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Don't forget to register for the next webcast What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2605 webcast, covering the top features from the releases 2603-2605. Register here.
  • Save the date for our next roadmap webcast on June 3, 4pm CET. Registration will be open soon.
  • Check the latest update of the APM link collection. 
  • You can explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.

entry.title: AVEVA PI Server Integration to SAP APM via thin-edge.io
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) automatically comes with Embedded IoT capabilities powered by Cumulocity. In our first post, we (the topic experts from Cumulocity) showed you how to get started by simply connecting your smartphone sensors. Then we proceeded to show how to connect an OPC-UA server. Today, we want to go the next step and present an integration to AVEVA PI Server, one of the most popular industrial data historians.

AVEVA PI Connector for Cumulocity

Industrial historians like AVEVA PI System are the backbone of operational data in many manufacturing and energy environments, storing decades of high-frequency time-series data from sensors, PLCs, and SCADA systems. However, that data often remains siloed — inaccessible to modern IoT platforms, cloud analytics, and digital twin solutions.

Unlocking this data opens the door to an entirely new class of use cases. Until now, many teams have been unable to act on the full potential of their Historian data simply because it never left the plant floor. Bringing AVEVA PI System data into Cumulocity changes that — enabling use cases that were previously out of reach:

  • Predictive process optimization — Feed live and historical tag data directly into ML models to anticipate process deviations before they impact production.
  • Richer operational insights — Surface time-series data in real-time dashboards and anomaly detection pipelines to give operators and engineers a clearer picture of what’s happening across their assets.
  • ML model training & inference — Use decades of high-fidelity sensor data to train models, then keep those models accurate with a continuous stream of live input signals.
  • Predictive maintenance & quality control — Drive maintenance scheduling and quality decisions from actual equipment behavior rather than fixed intervals or manual inspection.
  • OT/IT convergence — Break down the barrier between operational and enterprise systems, creating a unified data foundation for cross-functional teams.

This guide walks you through connecting an AVEVA PI System (Historian) to Cumulocity using a Python-based microservice and thin-edge. The integration works by running a lightweight Python microservice alongside thin-edge.io — Cumulocity’s open-source edge agent — which handles secure northbound communication, local buffering, and device management out of the box. Together, they bridge your PI Server’s Web API endpoint with Cumulocity’s cloud platform, translating PI tag data into Cumulocity measurements in real time.

By the end of this guide, you’ll have real-time industrial data flowing from your AVEVA PI System into Cumulocity, enabling dashboards, alerts, analytics, and integration with downstream IT systems — without disrupting your existing OT infrastructure.

Use Cases

This solution is ideal for:

  • Industrial IoT data ingestion - Bridge legacy AVEVA PI Systems to modern IoT platforms
  • Edge gateway integration - Deploy at the edge for local processing
  • SCADA to IoT bridging - Connect operational technology (OT) to IT systems
  • Real-time plant monitoring - Stream live process data to the cloud
  • Predictive maintenance - Feed AVEVA PI System data into analytics pipelines

What You’ll Build

A complete data pipeline that:

  1. Reads industrial data from AVEVA PI System via PI Web API
  2. Publishes data to a local MQTT broker
  3. Routes telemetry through thin-edge.io gateway
  4. Delivers data to Cumulocity IoT platform

Key Benefits:

  • Live configuration updates without service restarts
  • Edge-friendly deployment via Cumulocity Software Management
  • Remote configuration management from the cloud
  • Automatic configuration reload on changes

GitHub - Cumulocity-IoT/thin-edge-aveva-pi-connector

This repository contains a thinege plugin that connects to an AVEVA PI System using REST APIs, retrieves data at scheduled intervals, and integrates it with the Cumulocity platform via thin-edge

Architecture Overview

Here’s how the components work together:

The thin-edge Connector for AVEVA PI is deployed to a docker environment and is acting as a bridge between the PI Web API and MQTT Broker of thin-edge.io. thin-edge.io itself can be installed locally on the machine or also run as a container. It bridges the communication from MQTT to Cumulocity.

With Cumulocity Device Management thin-edge.io and the connector is fully remote managed including installation/deinstallation, software updates and configuration management.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have:

Infrastructure Requirements

  • A VM or edge device running on Linux
  • Docker installed and running
  • unzip package installed

thin-edge.io Requirements

  • thin-edge.io agent installed and running
  • Device connected to Cumulocity
  • Device registered as a thin-edge device
  • thin-edge Mosquitto MQTT broker running and accessible
  • thin-edge container plugin installed

AVEVA PI System Requirements

  • Access credentials for PI Web API
  • Network connectivity from edge device to AVEVA PI System
  • List of AVEVA PI tags/datapoints you want to monitor

Step-by-step Guide

Step 1: Prepare the Software Package

You have two options to obtain the software:

Option A: Download Pre-built Package

  1. Visit the GitHub Releases page
  2. Download the latest pi_connector_{version}.zip package
  3. Skip to Option B

Option B: Build from Source

  1. Clone the repository:

    git clone https://github.com/Cumulocity-IoT/thin-edge-aveva-pi-connector
cd thin-edge-aveva-pi-connector

  2. Create the deployable package:

    zip -r pi-historian-connector.zip \
         app.py \
         docker-compose.yaml \
         Dockerfile \
         requirements.txt

    This creates pi-historian-connector.zip excluding development files.

Step 2: Upload to Cumulocity Software Repository

  1. Log in to Cumulocity (Embedded IoT Platform as part of SAP APM)

  2. Navigate to Management → Software repository

  3. Click Add Software

  4. Fill in the details:

    NamePI Historian Connector
    Software Typecontainer-group
    Versione.g., 1.0.0
    FileUpload your .zip package

  5. Click Add Software to save

Step 3: Configure thin-edge.io

Register Configuration Files

The service uses two configuration files that need to be registered with thin-edge.io for remote management.

  1. Edit the thin-edge configuration plugin:

    sudo nano /etc/tedge/tedge-configuration-plugin.toml

  2. Add the following configuration entries:

    [[files]]
path = "/etc/tedge/tedge.toml"
type = "tedge.toml"

[[files]]
group = "tedge"
mode = 444
path = "/etc/tedge/plugins/tedge-log-plugin.toml"
type = "tedge-log-plugin"
user = "tedge"

[[files]]
path = "/etc/tedge/c8y/datapoints.json"
type = "pi_datapoints"
user = "tedge"
group = "tedge"
mode = 0o644

[[files]]
path = "/etc/tedge/c8y/pi_config.json"
type = "pi_config"
user = "tedge"
group = "tedge"
mode = 0o644

  3. Restart thin-edge services:

    sudo systemctl restart tedge-agent

Step 4: Create Configuration Files

4.1 Create PI System Configuration

Create a file named pi_config.json with your PI System connection details:

{
    "RECORDING_AT_TIME": "?time=",
    "POLL_INTERVAL": 90,
    "PI_USER": "your_pi_username",
    "PI_PASSWORD": "your_base64_encoded_password",
    "PI_URL": "https://pi-webapi.your-domain.com/piwebapi"
}

Configuration Parameters:


ParameterDescriptionExample
RECORDING_AT_TIMEPI API query parameter for time-based data"?time="
POLL_INTERVALData polling interval in seconds90
PI_USERPI Web API username"pi_readonly_user"
PI_PASSWORDPI Web API password (base64-encoded)"cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM="
PI_URLPI Web API endpoint URL"https://pi-server.local/piwebapi"

Tip: To encode your password in base64:

echo -n "your_password" | base64

4.2 Create Datapoints Configuration

Create a file named datapoints.json listing the PI tags you want to collect:

[
  "78FIQ301.A",
  "78FIC102.A",
  "REACTOR_TEMP_01",
  "FLOW_METER_MAIN"
]

Each entry should be a valid PI tag name from your PI System.

Step 5: Upload Configurations to Cumulocity

Now upload both configuration files to your thin-edge device:

  1. Navigate to your device in Cumulocity:

    • Go to Device Management → All devices
    • Select your thin-edge device

  2. Upload PI System configuration:

    • Click the Configuration tab
    • Click Add configuration snapshot
    • Select type: pi_config
    • Upload your pi_config.json file
    • Click Upload

  3. Upload datapoints configuration:

    • Click Add configuration snapshot again
    • Select type: pi_datapoints
    • Upload your datapoints.json file
    • Click Upload

Step 6: Deploy the Service

  1. Navigate to your thin-edge device:

    • Go to Device Management → All devices
    • Select your thin-edge device

  2. Install the software:

    • Click the Software tab
    • Click Install software
    • Search for “PI Historian Connector”
    • Select the version you uploaded
    • Click Install

  3. Monitor deployment:

    • Watch the Operations tab for deployment status
    • Wait for status to show Successful

  4. Verify the service is running:

    sudo docker ps | grep pi-historian

    You should see the container running.

Step 7: Verify Data Flow

Check Service Logs

sudo docker logs -f <container-name>

Look for messages indicating:

  • Successful connection to PI Web API
  • Data retrieval from PI tags
  • MQTT message publishing

Monitor MQTT Messages

mosquitto_sub -t 'te/device/main/#' -v

You should see telemetry data being published.

Check Cumulocity Measurements

  1. Navigate to your device in Cumulocity

  2. Click the Measurements tab

  3. Verify that PI tag data is appearing

Managing Your Deployment

Update Configuration

To modify polling intervals, add new tags, or change credentials:

  1. Edit your local pi_config.json or datapoints.json
  2. Go to Device Management → Your device → Configuration
  3. Upload the updated file
  4. The service automatically detects and applies changes without restart

Update the Service

To deploy a new version:

  1. Upload the new version to Software repository
  2. Go to Device Management → Your device → Software
  3. Select the new version and click Update

Troubleshooting

Service not starting:

sudo docker logs <container-name>

Check thin-edge connectivity:

sudo tedge connect c8y
sudo systemctl status tedge-agent

Verify MQTT broker:

sudo systemctl status mosquitto

Check configuration files:

cat /etc/tedge/c8y/pi_config.json
cat /etc/tedge/c8y/datapoints.json

Advanced Topics

Data Flow Example

PI Tag: REACTOR_TEMP_01 = 127.5°C
     │
     ▼
PI Historian Service
     │ (polls every 90s)
     ▼
MQTT Topic: te/device/main/m/reactor_temp
     │ {"temperature": 127.5}
     ▼
Cumulocity Measurement
     │
     ▼
Dashboard Visualization

Custom Tag Mapping

You can extend datapoints.json to include friendly names:

[
  {
    "tag": "78FIQ301.A",
    "name": "Flow Rate - Inlet 301",
    "unit": "m³/h"
  },
  {
    "tag": "78FIC102.A",
    "name": "Temperature - Reactor 102",
    "unit": "°C"
  }
]

Future Enhancements

Planned improvements include:

  • Batch data ingestion for high-frequency tags
  • Local buffering during network outages
  • Advanced tag transformations and calculations
  • Built-in metrics and observability
  • Kubernetes-native deployment options

Contributing

We welcome contributions! Visit the repository: GitHub - Cumulocity-IoT/thin-edge-aveva-pi-connector: This repository contains a thinege plugin that connects to an AVEVA PI System using REST APIs, retrieves data at scheduled intervals, and integrates it with the Cumulocity platform via thin-edge · GitHub

Ways to contribute:

  • Improve documentation - Help others understand better
  • Add features - Extend functionality
  • Fix bugs - Report or resolve issues
  • Enhance performance - Optimize code

Please open an issue or submit a pull request.

Additional Resources

  • Similar Integration: Node-RED based PI Historian integration
  • thin-edge.io Documentation: Overview | Thin-edge
  • Cumulocity Guides: Cumulocity documentation
  • AVEVA PI System: PI System™ | AVEVA

entry.title: Building an AI Agent for SAP Maintenance using MCP, CAP and Claude Desktop
entry.content:
From Transactions to Conversations: Connecting Claude Desktop to SAP S/4HANA via MCP

A few weekends ago I sat down with a question I've been carrying for months: how fast is generative AI actually going to change the way we interact with SAP? Not in the marketing sense. In the day-to-day, "open IW38, set the filters, export to Excel" sense.

I didn't want to write another opinion piece. I wanted to build something and see for myself.

This post is the result: a working proof of concept, two short videos of it running, and an honest reflection on what I think it means for everyone in the SAP world, users, developers, consultants, support teams.

 Why I Built This

The trigger was the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the open standard Anthropic released to let AI assistants talk to external systems through a clean, structured interface. The moment I understood how it worked, one thought wouldn't leave me alone: if Claude Desktop can call any tool, why is it still asking people to navigate transactions to get SAP data?

So I picked the SAP module I know best, Plant Maintenance, and built a bridge.

 What the Project Does

The proof of concept lets a user ask Claude Desktop questions like these, in natural language, and get answers pulled live from SAP S/4HANA:

  • "I have a meeting in 5 minutes about EDDY Pumps. Give me a quick summary of all open orders."
  • "Is there any order related to an oil leak? What's planned to fix it?"
  • "Equipment 10001949, does it have more than one open order? Show me all of them."
  • "Which orders have priority 1 in plant 1010?"

No SAP GUI. No Fiori. No transaction codes. No filter screens. The user just asks.

See It Running

I recorded two short videos showing the assistant pulling real data from S/4HANA:

Video 1 - Summary before a meeting:


Video 2 - Investigating a leak:

 The Architecture (Briefly)

 

For those who want the technical picture:

  • SAP CAP on Node.js exposes an MCP-compliant HTTP endpoint on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
    A small local script (mcp-stdio.js) acts as a stdio bridge between Claude Desktop and the BTP server
    The server queries SAP S/4HANA via OData (using SAP Business Accelerator Hub for the sandbox)
    Claude Desktop sees it as a standard MCP server with tools it can call

  •  

get_maintenance_orders
get_maintenance_order_detail
get_maintenance_order_operations

At runtime, Claude Desktop sees a small set of domain-specific tools exposed via MCP:

The full code, deploy steps, and configuration example are in the repo. Lessons learned (CAP routing quirks, Express middleware gotchas, why you need the stdio proxy at all) are documented there too.

  • Step 2 - configure Claude Desktop

    Open %APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json and add:

 

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sap-maintenance": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": [
        "C:\\path\\to\\mcp-maintenance-cap\\mcp-stdio.js"
      ]
    }
  }
}

 Now the Honest Part What This Could Mean for the SAP World

This is where I want to slow down. Because the demo is fun, but the implications are bigger.

For End Users
The maintenance manager who today depends on someone, or on remembering filter combinations she learned five years ago, to pull a report? She just asks. The supervisor who wanted to know which equipment has the most open notifications but never had time to learn the t-code? He just asks.

This is, unambiguously, a win for the user. The system finally bends to the human, instead of the other way around.

 For Developers and Consultants

Here's where it gets interesting, and where I find myself thinking out loud.

If a well-designed AI agent can answer most operational questions through natural language, what happens to the parts of our work that revolve around teaching users to navigate the system, building the 47th custom report, or writing the "how to filter IW38" page of a training manual?

I don't think those things disappear overnight. Enterprise software doesn't move at consumer-AI speed. But I do think the shape of the work changes, and it changes faster than most of us are planning for.

 For Configuration, Training, Projects, and Support

A few honest questions I'm sitting with:

  • If an AI agent can explain why an order is in a given status by reading the master data on the fly, how much classic L1/L2 support is still needed?
  • If users no longer need to learn screens, how much of a typical training plan survives?
  • If a consultant's value moves from "I know where the field is" to "I know how to design the agent and the data behind it," what does the next generation of SAP consultant look like?
  • When does this happen, 2 years, 5 years, 10 years? I genuinely don't know. Anyone who tells you they do is selling something.

 What's Next

The same pattern works for any SAP module. The maintenance use case was where I started because it's mine, but you can imagine equivalents for MM (stock and POs), SD (orders and deliveries), QM (inspections), or FI/CO (cost centers and postings). The architecture doesn't care which OData service is on the other end.

I'd love to see this turn into a community conversation. What modules would you try first? What does the "agent-first SAP" workflow look like in your area? Where do you see the real risks?

Try It, Break It, Tell Me About It
The full project code, deploy steps, lessons learned, and videos are open on GitHub:

https://github.com/bernardcaldas/mcp-maintenance-cap 

Suggestions, pull requests, and issues are very welcome on the repo. Critiques, corrections, and "you missed this" are equally welcome.

We're early. None of us has the full picture. But I'd rather build small things and learn in public than wait for someone else to define what AI + SAP looks like.

Thanks for reading.

entry.title: Breakdown Maintenance KPIs in SAP Plant Maintenance (PM)
entry.content:
In SAP Plant Maintenance, equipment reliability is critical for ensuring uninterrupted operations. During my work related to maintenance reporting, I became particularly interested in KPIs like MTTR, MTBR and Availability, and how they are calculated. This blog is an attempt to break down these concepts in a simple and practical way using a real example. Rather than just focusing on definitions, I wanted to understand how these KPIs behave with actual breakdown data and what insights they can provide in a real maintenance scenario. Frequent equipment breakdowns can lead to production losses and increased maintenance costs. Therefore, most organizations use Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to monitor equipment reliability. SAP PM supports the tracking of these KPIs through Maintenance Notifications and Orders when breakdown information is recorded accurately. These KPIs can be analyzed using standard SAP PM reports such as breakdown analysis report.

The key performance indicators include:

  1. MTTR – Mean Time to Repair
  2. MTBR – Mean Time Between Repairs
  3. MTBF – Mean Time Between Failures
  4. Equipment Availability
  5. Failure Rate 

This blog explains:

  1. Prerequisites required for Breakdown analysis
  2. How MTTR, MTBR, MTBF and Availability are calculated
  3. A practical example of reliability calculation
  4. Additional KPIs used in industry

1. Prerequisites required for Breakdown analysis

Before calculating any KPIs in SAP PM, I have noticed that the biggest challenge is not the calculation itself, but the quality of the data maintained in the system. Even small inaccuracies in breakdown timing can significantly impact the KPI results. To ensure meaningful analysis, the following data points must be properly maintained:

1.1. Equipment Master – Start-up Date

In the Equipment master, the start-up date field should be maintained. This is used to calculate the operating time and represents when the equipment started functioning. 

(Transaction: IE01/IE02)

1.2. Maintenance Notification – Breakdown Indicator

When a breakdown occurs, a maintenance notification should be created, where the breakdown indicator must be maintained. The Malfunction Start Date and Malfunction End Date should also be maintained.           

(Transaction: IW21/IW22)

Note: The Malfunction End Date can be updated during TECO, depending on system configuration.

1.3. Maintenance Order - TECO

After notification is created, a breakdown maintenance order is created to repair the equipment. Maintenance order confirmation and TECO should be done to the order. While performing TECO, Malfunction End Date or Malfunction hours should be entered.

(Transaction: IW31/IW32)

2. Practical Example – Compressor KPI Calculation

The following example walks through the calculation of all KPIs for the equipment called COMPRESSOR.

The start-up date of the compressor is on 02.02.2025. The first breakdown occurs on 10.05.2025 and ends on 11.05.2025. Similarly, the second breakdown occurs on 10.07.2025 and ends on 11.07.2025.

BREAK DOWN -1

Malfunction Start

10.05.2025

10:54:17

Malfunction End

11.05.2025

00:00:00

BREAK DOWN -2

Malfunction Start

10.07.2025

08:11:57

Malfunction End

11.07.2025

16:00:00

Step 1: Calculate Total Repair Time

So, the time taken to Repair is

Breakdown -1

=> 10:54:17 to 11:00:00 = 5 min 43 sec

=> 11:00:00 to 24:00:00 = 13 hours

So, it is 13:05:43,

13 + (5/60) + (43/3600) = 13 + 0.0833 + 0.0119

                                             = 13.095 = 13.10 hours

Breakdown - 2

From 10th July 08:11:57 to 11th July 08:11:57 = 24 hours

From 11th July 08:11:57 to 11th July 16:00:00 = 7 hours 48 min 03 sec

Total Downtime = 24h+ 7h 48m 03s = 31h 48m 03s

                                                                    = 31.80083 hours

So, the Total Repair Time = 13.10 + 31.80083

                                                = 44.90083 h ~ 44.90 h

Step 2: Calculate Operating Time (Time Between Breakdowns)

Gap before Breakdown 1 (Start-up → BD1 start)

            From 02.02.2025 to 10.05.2025 = 97 days

            Plus extra time = 10 h 54 m = 10.9 h

            Total = (97 x 24) + 10.9 = 2338.9 h

Gap between Breakdown 1 end and Breakdown 2 start

           From 11.05.2025 to 10.07.2025 = 60 days

           Extra time = 8h 11 m = 8.18 h

           Total = (60 x 24) + 8.18 = 1448.18 h

Total Operating Time

            2338.9 + 1448.18 = 3787.08 h

                                              = 3787.1 h

3. KPI Calculations

3.1. MTTR – Mean Time to Repair

MTTR is the average time required to repair equipment after a failure. From what I’ve observed, this KPI is often closely monitored by maintenance teams because it directly reflects how efficiently breakdowns are handled. A lower MTTR indicates a faster, more efficient maintenance team.

         MTTR = Total Repair Time / Number of Breakdowns

                              = 44.90 / 2

                      = 22.45 hours

Insight: On average, it takes 22.45 hours to restore the compressor after each failure.

3.2. MTBR – Mean Time Between Repairs

MTBR measures the average operating time between repair events, that is, from the end of one repair to the start of the next breakdown. In my perspective, this KPI is useful for understanding how long, the equipment typically runs before requiring maintenance again. So, a higher MTBR indicates better equipment reliability.

MTBR = Total Operating Time / Number of Breakdowns

     = 3787.1 / 2

     = 1893.55 h

Insight: On average, the compressor runs for ~1,893 hours between repair events.

3.3. MTBF – Mean Time Between Failures

MTBF measures the average time between the onset of one failure and the onset of the next. It is an uptime-based metric. In practice, MTBF and MTBR may appear similar when downtime is small compared to uptime. However, they are conceptually different: MTBF measures time between failure occurrences, while MTBR measures time between repair cycles. SAP PM standard reports may not always clearly distinguish these depending on data granularity. In frameworks that explicitly exclude downtime, MTBF and MTBR may differ. However, since repair time here is small relative to operating time, the practical difference is negligible.

MTBF = 3787.1 / 2

            = 1893.55 h

Insight: SAP PM aligns MTBF with uptime-based calculation. A higher MTBF is always desirable.

3.4. Equipment Availability

Availability represents the proportion of time the equipment is operational versus total time (operating + repair). It is one of the most important KPIs for assessing operational efficiency. From what I have observed is, that this KPI reflects how reliable the equipment is in supporting operations.

Availability = MTBR / (MTBR +MTTR)

              = 1893.55 / (1893.55 + 22.45) 

              = 0.9882

Availability Percentage = 0.9882 x 100

                                     = 98.82 %

Insight: The compressor is available 98.82% of the time, which indicates excellent performance.

3.5. Failure Rate

Failure Rate indicates how frequently failures occur per unit of operating time. A lower failure rate indicates better reliability. I have found that this KPI is helpful to quickly identify whether an asset is becoming more failure prone over time. 

Failure rate = Number of Failures / Operating time

              = 2 / 3787.1

              = 0.00053 failures / hour

Insight: The compressor fails approximately once every 1,893 operating hours. A lower failure rate indicates better reliability and fewer unexpected disruptions.

4. KPI Summary – Compressor

KPI

Formula

Result

Benchmark

MTTR

Total Repair Time / Breakdowns

22.45 h

Lower is better

MTBR

Operating Time / Breakdowns

1893.55 h

Higher is better

MTBF

Operating Time / Failures

1893.55 h

Higher is better

Availability

MTBR / (MTBR + MTTR)

98.82 %

Target ≥ 95 %

Failure Rate

Failures / Operating Time

0.000528 /h

Lower is better

5. Key Takeaways

  • From this analysis, one thing becomes very clear, accurate data entry in SAP PM is absolutely critical. If breakdown indicators or malfunction times are not maintained correctly, the KPIs can become misleading.
  • Another important observation is the difference between MTBF and MTBR. While they may produce similar values in SAP standard reports, understanding their conceptual difference is useful when comparing with industry benchmarks
  • In my view, KPIs like Availability and Failure Rate provide a quick and practical understanding of equipment performance. When these metrics are consistently tracked, they help teams move away from reactive maintenance towards a more proactive and preventive approach.

6. SAP Transaction Code (T-Code) Quick Reference

T-Code

Description

Purpose in Breakdown Analysis

IE01 / IE02

Create / Change Equipment Master

Maintain Start-Up Date

IW21 / IW22

Create / Change Notification

Set Breakdown Indicator, Malfunction Dates

IW31 / IW32

Create / Change Maintenance Order

Order Confirmation & TECO

MCI7 / MCJB / MCJC

Breakdown Analysis

Breakdown / MTTR / MTBF Analysis

7. Conclusion

By working with this example, it helped me better to understand how powerful breakdown analysis can be in SAP PM. Even with just two breakdown events, we can already see meaningful insights. In real-world scenarios, where equipment data is collected over longer periods, these KPIs become even more valuable.

From my perspective, the key takeaway is simple. It is that if the data is maintained correctly in notifications and orders, SAP PM can provide very strong support for improving equipment reliability and reducing downtime.

entry.title: Transforming SAP APM Documentation for a Global Audience
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) documentation is enhanced to make it more accessible and multilingual. Building on earlier enhancements like SAP Companion and video tutorials, the MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) marks a leap forward — ensuring accuracy, consistency, and up to date content across all delivery channels.

What This Means for You

  • Always current: Documentation updates happen at the same pace as product changes. Documentation pages available in 39+ languages.
  • Multilingual access: Read content in your preferred language without waiting for translations.
  • Ease of use: Switch between languages anytime, with English always available as the official reference.

Key Enhancements

  • March 2603 release
    • SAP Companion In‑App Help: Context‑sensitive panels, orientation snippets, and direct links to the SAP Help Portal.
    • Video Tutorials: Step‑by‑step guidance to help users learn processes while working.
  • Machine Translation Evolution
  • February 2025: Machine Translation on the Fly (MT on the Fly) introduced dynamic multilingual documentation, allowing users to access content in their preferred language.
  • April 2604: Building on this foundation, MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) delivered a leap forward. MTOTF enhances accuracy and consistency across all delivery channels, expanding usability worldwide. With this, users will gain faster access to reliable documentation in their preferred language.

Disclaimer: Keep in mind that this is an AI automatic translation tool based on the English Language. Be cautious with the translated content as it may not be perfect. When in doubt, consult the original English text.  

 How to Access Multilingual Documentation

  1. Open the SAP Help Portal → SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal
  2. Select Your Language
    • Use the language dropdown at the top of the page (next to “Version: Cloud | English”).
    • Click the arrow and choose from available languages drop-down menu.
    • Select the desired language such as Arabic, Chinese, French, German, Spanish, and more.
  3. Switch Back to English Anytime

Conclusion

The Machine Translation On-the-Fly allows instant translation in 40 languages for SAP APM Help Portal content, enhancing your experience as you can now choose your preferred language. 

With MTOTF, SAP APM documentation is becoming more connected, consistent, and globally accessible.

📌See Also

  • SAP Help Portal Content with Machine Translation On-the-Fly
  • Read the Right Docs: sIoT vs. eIoT in SAP Asset Performance Management

entry.title: SAP APM Visual Inspection: Edge AI Inferencing via thin-edge.io
entry.content:
This article walks through a practical example of running AI/ML inference directly on an edge device — a thermal analytics use case for visual inspection. The goal is to show how the edge inferencing approach actually works in practice, end to end, from capturing a thermal image to triggering a downstream action in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) Visual Inspection.

For broader context on how AI/ML models are trained, managed, and deployed on Cumulocity (covering both cloud and edge), readers can refer to the foundation article: Bringing AI/ML to Cumulocity — Cloud and Edge Inferencing.

The Use Case

The scenario is straightforward: a thermal infrared camera is connected to Cumulocity via thin-edge.io. The camera continuously captures thermal images of equipment, and the goal is to detect hotspots — regions where the temperature exceeds a defined threshold.

When a hotspot is detected, an event should be raised in Cumulocity with the relevant details (bounding box, temperature values, annotated image), and the same information should also be sent to a downstream service — in this example, the SAP APM Visual Inspection — for further processing.

The advantage of this integration is that the inspection results land directly in the hands of field workers and inspection engineers, where they can be reviewed, validated, and acted upon as part of the existing maintenance process. Instead of AI insights staying isolated in a separate system, they become part of the standard inspection workflow — closing the gap between automated detection and real-world action.

This is a classic computer vision workflow, and a good fit for edge inferencing. The thermal images are large, the analysis needs to happen close to the source, and only the meaningful results need to be sent upstream.

The Setup

This demo shows how thermal images captured from a thermal infrared camera — such as the Optris Xi 410 or any similar radiometric camera — connected to Cumulocity via thin-edge.io can be analyzed locally using an AI model. In this setup, a Raspberry Pi running thin-edge.io acts as the edge device.

Modern thermal cameras produce radiometric images — images that not only show temperature variation visually but also contain per-pixel temperature data. This makes them especially well-suited for ML-based hotspot detection.

For this demo, synthetic radiometric images generated by a simulator are used as input. Eight thermal frames are fed into the pipeline — most show normal temperature variation, while the last two contain regions of significantly higher temperature. The data format matches exactly what a typical thermal camera SDK would produce, so the pipeline is camera-ready and can be connected to a real camera with minimal changes.

How It Works on the Edge

For edge inferencing, a generic ONNX Pipeline Runner is used in combination with Cumulocity. The runner is an open-source solution and will be made available through a GitHub repository. It is packaged as a standard Debian package, installed once on the device through Cumulocity's Software Repository, and then it acts as a reusable inference engine on the edge.

The runner does not know anything about thermal imaging, anomaly detection, or any other use case. It simply executes a three-step pipeline — pre-processing, ONNX inference, post-processing — and expects these three components as configuration files on the device. These files are managed centrally through Cumulocity's Configuration Repository.

For this thermal analytics use case, the three components look like this:

  • Pre-processing — Reads the thermal frame (a radiometric array) and prepares it as input for the ONNX model. When connecting a real camera, this script is simply updated to read from the camera SDK instead of the simulated files. 
  • ONNX inference — A lightweight hotspot detector runs on the input frame. It scans for regions where the temperature exceeds the defined threshold and outputs a heat grid indicating where the hotspots are.
  • Post-processing — Interprets the model output. If temperatures are within range, nothing happens. If the threshold is exceeded, the script generates an event in Cumulocity with the bounding box, temperature values, and an annotated image. 

A Cumulocity microservice then triggers a REST call to the SAP APM Visual Inspection with the corresponding JSON payload.

Closing the Loop

Once the pipeline is deployed, here is how the end-to-end flow looks in action.

Step 1 — Inference on the edge

The runner picks up the thermal frame, runs it through the ONNX model, and identifies hotspots locally on the device. No data leaves the edge unless something meaningful is detected.

Step 2 — Event raised in Cumulocity

When the temperature threshold is exceeded, the post-processing step generates an event in Cumulocity. The event carries the bounding box, temperature values, and the annotated thermal image.

Step 3 — Result available in SAP APM Visual Inspection Manager

The same event triggers a REST call with the structured JSON payload to SAP APM Visual Inspection Manager. From here, field workers and inspection engineers can review the results and act on them as part of the regular maintenance workflow.

The inferencing happens entirely on the edge. Only the meaningful results — events and structured payloads — are sent to the cloud, keeping bandwidth low and ensuring real-time responsiveness.

The Power of the Generic Runner

One of the strongest aspects of this design is reusability. Today, the device runs a thermal hotspot detector. Tomorrow, the same device could run an anomaly detection model — without rebuilding or redeploying anything.

The pipeline runner stays exactly the same. Only the configuration files change: a different pre-processor (to read sensor data instead of thermal frames), a different ONNX model (trained for anomaly detection), and a different post-processor (to emit anomaly events). All of this is pushed through Cumulocity's Configuration Repository — no SSH, no code changes, no downtime on the device.

The same applies to model updates. As new training data comes in from the field, the model can be retrained externally, exported as a new ONNX file, and pushed down to the edge through Configuration Management. The runner picks up the new model automatically.

Wrapping Up

This article showed how the generic edge inferencing approach plays out in a real visual inspection scenario. The thermal analytics use case is just one example — the same pipeline can be adapted to anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, or any other ML use case that benefits from running close to the data.

The key idea remains simple: train your models using your own preferred tools, manage them through Cumulocity's existing repositories, and let the edge runner handle the inference.

entry.title: How to Send Email for Maintenance Orders Generated by Maintenance Plans in S/4HANA Cloud 2602
entry.content:
In S/4HANA Cloud 2602, there are multiple ways to trigger email notifications when preventive maintenance orders are generated from maintenance plan.

This blog outlines two approaches: one configuration driven (recommended) and one extensibility driven to help you choose the right solution based on your requirements and Clean Core strategy.

Business Requirement

Alert relevant users to their company email address when a maintenance order is generated from a maintenance plan so that they can review and take action promptly.

Option 1: Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders (Recommended)

The standard and Clean Core–aligned approach is to use the Fiori app:

F4989 – Flexible Workflow for Maintenance Orders:

🔧Configuration Steps

  • Define Global Preconditions Set workflow trigger conditions such as:
    • Order Type (Preventive)
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (ensures trigger from IP10/IP30)
    • Planning Plant / Planner Group (optional filters)
  • Add Workflow Step
    • Step Type: Notification Only
    • Recipients:
      • Partner on order
      • Planner group
      • Person responsible
      • Specific users
    • Execution:
      • Enable auto-completion in My Inbox
  • Email Notification Setup:
    • Use Fiori app: Manage Email Templates
    • Copy standard template: SWT_CRT_NOTIFY_RECIPIENTS
    • Follow naming convention: YY_02000019_CRT_10
    • Maintain template content:
      • Subject: Alert: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} from PM is generated
      • Body: Maintenance Order {{OrderNumber}} has been generated via Maintenance Plan for Technical Object {{TechnicalObject}}. Please review your technical log.

Prerequisites:

  • User email maintained in:
    • Maintain Business Users
    • Manage Business Partners
  • Preventive order type:
    • Assigned to a Phase Model Profile
    • Workflow trigger based on status change (e.g., Approval)

Key Benefits

  • Fully aligned with SAP Clean Core principles
  • Configuration-driven (no coding required)
  • Business-maintainable and audit-ready
  • Upgrade-safe and scalable

Option 2: BAdI-Based Enhancement (Advanced Scenario)

For scenarios requiring more advanced logic, you can use ABAP Cloud extensibility.

🔧Technical Approach

Implement the cloud-released BAdI: BADI_EAM_MO_CH_BEF_SAVE

Trigger Logic:

  • Execute logic when:
    • Order Type = Preventive
    • Maintenance Plan ≠ empty (indicates IP10/IP30 origin)
    • Optional filters:
      • Planning Plant
      • Planner Group

Email Implementation

  • Use modern email API: CL_BCS_MAIL_MESSAGE

Key Implementation Steps

  • Set sender: .set_sender()
  • Add recipients dynamically: .add_recipient() (e.g., fetch from I_MaintenanceOrder CDS view)
  • Define subject: .set_subject()
  • Send email asynchronously: .send_asynchronous()
  • Helps avoid transactional impact or rollback issues

Key Benefits

  • Full flexibility in:
    • Additional Custom logic as per business needs
    • Recipient determination
    • Email content

Considerations

  • Requires ABAP Cloud (Tier 1 extensibility)
  • Higher development and maintenance effort
  • Needs governance to stay compliant with Clean Core strategy

Recommendation

  • Use Flexible Workflow (Option 1) as the default approach
  • Consider BAdI (Option 2) only when:
    • Workflow limitations prevent required logic
    • Advanced recipient or filtering logic is needed

Final Thoughts

  • For most use cases, Flexible Workflow provides a robust, scalable, and upgrade-safe solution aligned with SAP’s Clean Core strategy.
  • BAdI-based enhancement should be used selectively for advanced scenarios where additional flexibility is required.

Hope this helps!
